title: Consumer Spending Is Up, But That Signal No Longer Means What It Once Did
gist: Canadian real consumer spending is growing in line with its 10-year average, but the growth is no longer backed by income for 80% of households — it is increasingly funded by savings drawdown, rising asset values, and borrowing, leaving the middle 60% and bottom 20% financially fragile even as headline spending looks resilient.

insights.claim: Canadian real consumer spending rose about 2% in Q1 2026, in line with Canada's ten-year average and with the US, and per-capita spending has grown for six straight quarters, which historically would have signaled healthy household finances but no longer does.
insights.speaker: Terence Smith
insights.so_what: Investors and business planners should not read headline Canadian consumption strength as a proxy for household health; the composition of who is spending and how it's funded has decoupled from the aggregate number.
insights.quote: In the past, signals like these usually meant households were in good shape and buying more. But resilience is not durability.

insights.claim: Outside the top 20% of Canadian earners, spending growth is not coming from income; it is funded by drawing down savings, rising portfolio/asset values, and increased borrowing, while services (especially financial services tied to borrowing and asset fees) drive most of the growth and durable goods like cars, furniture and appliances are declining.
insights.speaker: Terence Smith
insights.so_what: Consumer-facing companies should distinguish spending that reflects genuine demand strength from spending propped up by finite balance-sheet buffers, which is more vulnerable to a shock.
insights.locator: Households are covering their spending by relying more on savings

insights.claim: The middle 60% of Canadian earners — traditionally the bedrock of consumer demand — now have savings and balance-sheet trends moving in the direction of the bottom 20% rather than the top 20%, raising the question of whether their spending eventually follows.
insights.speaker: Terence Smith
insights.so_what: A deterioration in the middle 60%'s spending would hit the mass-market consumer base most businesses are built around; this cohort is the one to watch for an inflection.
insights.locator: The strain has reached the mass market

themes.theme: Who is actually paying for the spending increase

insights.claim: Between 2021 and 2025, Canadian households across income tiers raised spending by similar proportions (27% for the lowest band, 19% for the middle, 24% for the top), but income growth only fully covered the increase for the top 20% (106% coverage); the middle 60% covered just 57 cents of every new dollar spent from income, and the bottom 20% covered almost none, with income up only 3%.
insights.speaker: Terence Smith
insights.so_what: Similar-looking spending growth across income tiers masks vastly different funding sources; only the top quintile's consumption is backed by earned income, meaning ~80% of Canadian consumer demand rests on non-income supports that can reverse.
insights.locator: Canadians Are Spending More Than They Earn

insights.claim: Between 2021 and 2025 the middle 60% of Canadian households moved from a modest positive savings position to spending more than their income, a deterioration of roughly $7,000 per household, while the lowest 20% fell further into negative savings, with annual savings declining by roughly $15,000 per household.
insights.speaker: Terence Smith
insights.so_what: These are concrete magnitude estimates of household financial strain that can be used to size downside risk to Canadian discretionary spending in future quarters.
insights.locator: The Middle Is Relying More on Savings

insights.claim: 41% of Canadians say they do not expect to save, or expect to save less, over the next six months per BCG's Global Consumer Radar Survey, a level slightly above where it stood in September 2024, indicating the savings erosion trend is not abating.
insights.speaker: Terence Smith
insights.so_what: A forward-looking survey signal corroborating the historical savings data suggests the funding gap for consumer spending is likely to widen rather than close in the near term.

themes.theme: Asset gains and borrowing are propping up spending unevenly

insights.claim: Rising asset valuations grew total assets by 13% to 26% for the top 80% of Canadian households between 2021 and 2025, giving them confidence and, for some, additional equity to borrow against, while the bottom 20% saw total assets shrink by 2%, leaving the households with the fewest buffers unable to benefit from the asset rally.
insights.speaker: Terence Smith
insights.so_what: Asset-price-driven consumption support is bifurcated by wealth; a market pullback would remove a spending prop mainly from the households that arguably need it least, but the households most exposed (bottom 20%) already have no cushion at all.
insights.locator: Asset Gains Are Sustaining Spending, But Not Equally

insights.claim: The middle 60% of Canadian households saw the fastest growth in liabilities of any income tier, up 22%, with much of their net wealth trapped in illiquid assets like homes, leaving them exposed to rising debt-service costs; the bottom 20% added 17% to liabilities against a shrinking asset base, cutting net wealth by roughly $35,000 per household.
insights.speaker: Terence Smith
insights.so_what: Rising middle-class liabilities plus illiquid net wealth make Canadian consumer demand sensitive to interest-rate moves; this is a concrete household-leverage figure to track alongside Bank of Canada policy.
insights.locator: Borrowing Is Filling More of the Gap

insights.claim: Canadian equity valuations sit near a 25-year high relative to the size of the economy while five-year Government of Canada bond yields have risen about 40 basis points in recent months; if asset values soften while borrowing costs climb, the households with the thinnest financial buffers have the least room to absorb the shock.
insights.speaker: Terence Smith
insights.so_what: This is a specific macro trigger to watch: a simultaneous equity drawdown and rate-driven refinancing squeeze would hit the leveraged middle and low-income cohorts hardest and could turn a currently 'resilient' spending picture negative quickly.
insights.locator: What This Means for Business Leaders

insights.claim: Canada no longer has a single 'average consumer' to plan around: the top fifth is pulling away on income, assets and balance-sheet strength while everyone else leans on thinning supports, so business growth plans need to be built around a more uneven, financially constrained consumer.
insights.speaker: Terence Smith
insights.so_what: Consumer companies should stop using blended national averages for demand planning and instead model income-tier-specific trajectories.
insights.locator: What This Means for Business Leaders

insights.claim: As financial pressure moves up the income spectrum, the middle class is expected to become more value-oriented and selective, making larger pack sizes and bulk options increasingly important value drivers for low- and middle-income consumers on everyday purchases.
insights.speaker: Terence Smith
insights.so_what: Retailers and CPG companies should expand value-format and bulk offerings ahead of a broader middle-class trade-down.
insights.locator: The middle may become even more value-oriented

insights.claim: As more Canadian spending is supported by borrowing, category demand is expected to become more sensitive to interest rates and credit availability, with automotive, luxury goods, dining and pet care flagged as categories showing the largest spending differences across income groups and thus most exposed to a credit tightening.
insights.speaker: Terence Smith
insights.so_what: Leadership teams in these specific sectors should stress-test demand against tighter credit conditions rather than assume current growth persists.

insights.claim: Because much household wealth sits in illiquid holdings like homes, asset gains are not translating evenly into spending power; if higher oil prices feed into broader inflation, consumers may struggle to keep up even with elevated asset prices, increasing the need for financing, trade-in, or deferred-payment options.
insights.speaker: Terence Smith
insights.so_what: Businesses should prepare flexible payment/financing offers as an oil-driven inflation shock is flagged as a plausible near-term risk to discretionary demand.
insights.locator: Wealth will not translate evenly into spending
